locked_facts: Relation between focal length and radius of curvature: f = R/2
Magnification: m = -v/u (negative m means inverted image)
Sign convention: all distances measured from pole; direction of incident light is positive
Concave mirror: f is negative (converging); Convex mirror: f is positive (diverging)
For concave mirror, real images form when object is beyond F; virtual image when object is between P and F
truth_statements: The mirror formula 1/v + 1/u = 1/f applies to both concave and convex mirrors when the new Cartesian sign convention is used
The focal length of a spherical mirror is half of its radius of curvature
A concave mirror forms a real and inverted image when the object is placed beyond the focus
A convex mirror always forms a virtual, erect, and diminished image regardless of object position
The magnification produced by a spherical mirror is the ratio of image height to object height and equals -v/u

aha_moment.what_to_show: Place an object at 2F of a concave mirror. The image forms at 2F on the same side — same size, real, and inverted (m = -1). Now move the object slightly closer to F. The image shoots far away and becomes hugely magnified. Move the object just past F to between F and P — the image flips to behind the mirror, becoming virtual and erect. This dramatic transition at F is the aha moment: F is the dividing line between real and virtual images.
aha_moment.visual_emphasis: Show three rays (parallel, through centre, through focus) converging to form the image. As the object crosses F, the reflected rays diverge and the image jumps behind the mirror. Highlight m changing sign at F.

simulation_states.label: Object beyond 2F — real, inverted, diminished image
simulation_states.description: Concave mirror with object placed beyond 2F (centre of curvature). Three standard rays drawn: parallel ray reflects through F, ray through C reflects back on itself, ray through F reflects parallel. Image forms between F and 2F — real, inverted, smaller than object.
simulation_states.emphasis: When the object is far from the mirror, the image is closer and smaller. All three rays converge at the image point.

simulation_states.label: Object at 2F — image at 2F, same size
simulation_states.description: Object placed at the centre of curvature (2F). Image forms at 2F on the same side. Image is real, inverted, and same size as the object. m = -1.
simulation_states.emphasis: This is the special symmetric case: object and image are equidistant from the mirror. Magnification is exactly -1.

simulation_states.label: Object between F and P — virtual, erect, magnified image
simulation_states.description: Object placed between F and P. Reflected rays diverge — they appear to come from behind the mirror. Image is virtual, erect, and magnified. This is how a concave mirror works as a magnifying mirror.
simulation_states.emphasis: When the object crosses F toward the mirror, the image type changes from real to virtual. The focal point is the critical boundary.

simulation_states.label: Interactive — move the object
simulation_states.description: Slider to move the object from beyond 2F to between P and F. Watch the image position, size, and nature change in real time. The mirror formula values update live.
simulation_states.emphasis: Observe: as the object approaches F, the image moves to infinity. Past F, the image jumps behind the mirror. Try the convex mirror too — the image is always virtual, erect, and diminished.

common_misconceptions.belief: Convex mirrors can form real images
trigger_phrases: convex se real image banta hai
common_misconceptions.correction: A convex mirror ALWAYS forms a virtual, erect, and diminished image regardless of where the object is placed. Only concave mirrors can form real images (when the object is beyond F).
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show convex mirror with object at any distance — the image is always behind the mirror, virtual, erect, and smaller. Contrast with concave mirror forming a real image.

common_misconceptions.belief: The image disappears when the object is at the focus
common_misconceptions.correction: When the object is at the focus of a concave mirror, reflected rays become parallel and the image forms at infinity — it does not disappear. The image is real, inverted, and highly magnified (theoretically infinite size).
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show rays from object at F reflecting parallel to each other. Explain that the image is at infinity, not absent. Show that moving the object slightly from F creates a very large, distant image.

common_misconceptions.belief: Sign convention does not matter — just use magnitudes
common_misconceptions.correction: The new Cartesian sign convention is essential. Without it, the mirror formula gives wrong results. Distances in the direction of incident light are positive, opposite are negative. Object distance u is always negative (object on left). The sign of v tells you whether the image is real (negative) or virtual (positive).
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show the same numerical problem solved with and without sign convention. Demonstrate that ignoring signs gives the wrong image position and nature.

legend.meaning: Pole — centre of the mirror surface
Centre of curvature — centre of the sphere of which the mirror is a part
Principal focus — midpoint between P and C
Image distance from pole (negative = real, positive = virtual)
